By Shawn Twing
There’s an old saying — if you want to catch �sh, �sh where the
�sh are. Obvious, right? Let’s talk about where the �sh are online.
There are literally hundreds of advertising platforms you could
use to drive paid traf�c to your MPPS. We’re going to focus only
on two — Google AdWords and Facebook. Why only two when we
have so many choices?
Reason #1 — Reach
Google AdWords (search), the Google Display Network (for our
purposes, retargeting), and Facebook have been estimated to
reach more than 85% of web users.
In 2017, 60% of digital advertising budgets were spent in AdWords
and Facebook.
Reason #2 — Ease of Use
It’s possible to setup AdWords and Facebook advertising
accounts in minutes. If you already have these accounts setup —
Fish Where the Fish Are
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 10 --

great. If not, setup won’t be a barrier to entry.
However, ease of use does not necessarily equate to quality of
output. There are thousands of ways to use AdWords and
Facebook incorrectly. The tools are agnostic. The strategies you’ll
learn in the upcoming lessons will show you how to focus your
efforts with these two platforms to get the best results.
Reason #3 — Quality Reporting
Google AdWords and Facebook both have excellent reporting. The
way you’ll learn to setup your campaigns also will integrate
perfectly with Google Analytics, which is exceptionally powerful
for data analysis and it’s 100% free.
Reason #4 — Virtuous Cycles
Both Google and Facebook reward quality.
Neither is a straight auction format. When you understand what
each platform wants for its users, construct your campaigns that
way, and then use the feedback each platform gives to make
improvements. Over time you will pay less money for more traf�c
(and the quality of that traf�c will improve as well).
How to do this is the subject of the next two lessons.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 10 --

Reason #5 — Focus
We’re far less likely to take action when we have too many choices
(Barry Schwartz calls this the Paradox of Choice).
Narrowing our options to two platforms encourages fast, focused
action (and in lesson seven we’ll learn how to interpret results
quickly and accurately so we can scale, which is the subject of
lesson eight).
In my experience, the number of options available for traf�c
generation creates a peculiar type of procrastination. It’s so easy
to jump from one platform to the next that nothing has a real
opportunity to gain traction.
Here’s an excerpt from an article I wrote about this:
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 10 --

Bruce Lee Can Help You Transform Your Tra�c Strategy
Martial artist, actor, teacher, and philosopher Bruce Lee died
seventeen years before the debut of the world wide web, yet he may
have given the best advice — ever – for creating and executing an
e�ective tra�c generation strategy.
While Lee was best known for his movie roles, his accomplishments in
the martial arts are also legendary. From his background in Wing
Chun Kung Fu, he developed an entirely new interpretation of Kung
Fu he named Jeet Kune Do that emphasized e�ciency, directness, and
simplicity. Lee’s relentless focus on “hacking away at the unessential”
gives us a powerful foundational principle we can use immediately.
How do you “hack away at the unessential”? The answer can be found
in a quote from Bruce Lee which is the secret to creating a sustainable,
predictable, and scalable tra�c engine for your business. Lee said:
“I fear not the man who has practiced 10,000 kicks once, but I fear
the man who has practiced one kick 10,000 times.”
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 10 --

To understand why this is so powerful, let’s compare and contrast a
typical tra�c generation scenario with a strategy informed by Bruce
Lee’s single-minded focus.
Strategy #1 — Variety is the Spice of Life
This strategy is all about action spread across di�erent opportunities.
Create an AdWords account, setup retargeting, create a few search
campaigns, try some banner ads on the Google Display Network, add
some video advertising through YouTube, setup a Facebook
advertising account, then LinkedIn, Twitter, Instagram, Pinterest,
Reddit, maybe add some third-party services like Sitescout, Adroll,
and Perfect Audience … and on and on it goes. There’s always
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 10 --

something new and exciting to try, new opportunities and
approaches, and certainly new products to learn the newest “secret”.
The fundamental problem with this approach is that e�ort is spread
too thin. By necessity, we become “dabblers” to borrow a phrase from
Mastery, by George Leonard. We never have the joy — or the business
impact — of truly mastering our craft. The often-used metaphor is
light — when di�used (think lightbulb) it will illuminate a room,
when magni�ed (think laser) it’ll burn through steel.
Strategy #2 — The Bruce Lee Method
Instead of looking for opportunities, the Bruce Lee method focuses
relentlessly on results. To use this method, we need to ask a better
question. Instead of “what could we do?”, we ask “what should we
do?” The di�erence cannot be overstated.
What could we do assumes that time, energy, and dollars are in�nite
and no prioritization is required. If that’s the case, we could do
anything and it wouldn’t matter. We have all the time in the world
and money is no object.
However, for everyone I know, time, energy, and dollars are �nite
which means we need to establish priorities. For example, if the
priority is to generate 150 leads per day for a marketing funnel at a
cost of $12 or less per lead, the next question we have to ask is “what is
the simplest, most direct, and most e�cient way to achieve that
goal?” In my experience, that’ll quickly and dramatically limit the
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 10 --

options available.
Once those options have been identi�ed, then it’s a matter of
practicing the one kick 10,000 times. If YouTube pre-roll videos appear
to be the best vehicle for reaching your goal, don’t dabble with them —
become a master. Learn everything you can about them, know your
audience inside and out, investigate the tools and resources available
(and experiment with them) look at your metrics daily (hourly!), study
your results, form and test hypotheses until you know that source of
tra�c and its impact on your business like the back of your hand.
Then — and only then — move on to the next source of tra�c and
repeat the process.
One amazing bene�t of this approach is that each new method
becomes easier to understand and integrate. (If there’s any “secret” to
my success in tra�c generation it’s that I have internalized the
fundamental principles of my �eld and that allows me to understand
new developments faster and at a deeper level than most people. It’s
not magic, it’s experience.)
But wait — what about the dangers of putting all of our eggs in one
basket. Anyone who has spent a lot of time and money generating
tra�c has had an important source of tra�c disappear overnight.
Facebook’s often inexplicable (and irrevocable) account shutdowns
are the most recent and dramatic form of this, and it happens
elsewhere as well.
I believe part of the solution is focusing our attention vertically into
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 10 --

methods �rst, and horizontally across platforms second.
For example, if paid search marketing is your starting point, get
something working well on Google and then replicate that (and make
the necessary tweaks) on Bing. If you’re reaching a professional
audience on Facebook, reach the same audience next on LinkedIn. If a
content strategy is working for you on Facebook, move on to services
like OutBrain. Banner advertising on the Google Display Network?
Sitescout next. Keep adding layers thematically and continually
move along the road to mastery.
Reason #6 — Synergy / Leverage
Google (search) and Facebook reach different audiences with
completely different mindsets (more on those differences later).
However, they both allow for behaviorally-based engagement with
audiences which means we can compound our results
signi�cantly, without a lot of extra effort. That’s what leverage is
about — a little extra input to produce a lot of potential output
(you’re going to learn how to do this in lesson six).
OK, now that we know where we’re going to focus, the next three
lessons are going to explain how to do that.
Lesson 3 explains the high-level differences between Google
(search) and Facebook, and how we’re going to use an SOI-
inspired approach to get the best out of each.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 10 --

Lesson 4 will go deeper into Google (search), and lesson 5 will go
deep into Facebook.
Even if you already plan to use only one platform, I encourage you
to read through both of those lessons. They’re less about the
individual platforms and more about the mindsets that bring out
the best in each one, and those mindsets cross-pollinate.
Later, when we add the magic of behaviorally-based retargeting it
will help to understand what Google and Facebook want, because
you’ll be using both.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 10 --
